story:
My neuropractioner, Dr. Mercury Pope, calls my despair a waste of time and reaches for the Helmet Doctor, the glossy headgear every clinic swears will fix you right quick. I tell him I’m against editing. He pauses, studies his Waffelpad, and asks how long I’ve lived in the Arcology. I tell him I was born here in the Lower Third. He admits neuropractioners are new, the bugs aren’t all worked out, and technology always runs a step ahead of us—then says the procedure is about as safe as current practice can make it. His office looks and tastes metallic, all brushed steel and copper accents, the air itself faintly tinny. Only a brittle bonsai beside the gleaming helmet looks alive, and even that seems doubtful.

Neurocrisp transformers like the one inside that helmet can clip out unwanted thoughts and memories, or swap them for edited versions. People I know swear it changed their lives. But sometimes a neuropractioner trims too much, or comes in sour and edits where they shouldn’t. The accidents are rare, but they exist. I don’t want to be one.

“So what’s the root of your problem?” he asks. I hesitate, because what I’m about to say is almost taboo. “I’m lonely.” He looks at me as if I’m joking, high, or broken in some new way. In the Arcology—one megacity shaped like a pyramid, thirty million people sealed inside nanotube skin built to resist monsoons and firestorms—loneliness is hard to explain. The hive model needs every cell doing its part. We pride ourselves on not being barbaric anymore—no executions, no lifelong prisons. Corrective methods keep citizens useful. Still, the ones who pull away don’t last long. Some people whisper the Garda quietly take care of them, but it’s only a rumor. Either way, admitting loneliness sounds a lot like announcing your separation from the hive.

“You’re not partnered,” he says. No. My ex, Alice, a human-android hybrid, left me for a pure android so lifelike you’d never know. It hurt, but my emptiness existed before her. He suggests a Komfortroboter. He even uses the word miracle. Then he asks if I want a new partner. I tell him no. I have friends. I just don’t want to be with anyone. He blinks. “And yet you say you’re lonely?” I nod.

He offers editing again. I refuse. He asks why I came. I don’t know. We end in silence. I agree to next week’s session, though I doubt it will help.

I ride three express elevators down to the Lower Third. On the way to my pod I cross thousands of people: uniforms, costumes, faces with those Arcology smiles and dead eyes. Some nod. Most don’t. I pass the recreation plaza and the mini-atrium with its perfect little bonsai forest and laugh. It’s touching, how hard we try, and a little ridiculous. By the time I reach my door, I’m laughing so intensely I worry the neighbors will call the Garda.

Because it hits me: I am not lonely for people. I am lonely for the Earth. For Mother Earth I don’t even know, sealed off beyond the Arcology’s impermeable skin. I was born inside. I will likely die inside. What I miss isn’t company. It’s the outside itself.
